title: Spread and standard deviation
intro: The "Slice" chain has grown to two locations. The average salary at both is exactly 35k: at pizzeria A everyone earns roughly the same, at pizzeria B some earn much less, some much more. Same mean — completely different teams. To catch that difference you need a second characteristic: how far the values scatter around the center.

beats.text: Two pizzerias, both with a mean salary of exactly 35k — the dashed line stands in the same place in both rows. But the points of pizzeria B are scattered much wider than A's. To the right of each row is its σ, the standard deviation.
The means of A and B are identical. The spread is not.
beats.predict: Press "shrink B" — the points of team B will slide toward the center. What do you think happens to B's mean and to its σ?
beats.reveal: B's mean stays at 35 (we shrink symmetrically toward the center), while σ drops — and team B starts to look like the steady team A. Press "stretch B" — σ grows back. The mean never moves.

beats.text: That is what σ is for: A and B share a mean, but the teams are different. σ catches what the mean cannot see — how widely the values diverge around the center. Small σ — everyone huddles near the middle; large σ — the spread is wide.

Here is how σ is computed: take each value's deviation from the mean, square it (so minuses do not cancel pluses), average — that is the variance — then take the square root to get back to money units. Because of the squaring, rare far-away values weigh more and noticeably inflate σ.

σ has the same weakness as the mean: it is built around the mean and is therefore sensitive to outliers — one director inflates not only the mean but σ too. That is why spread has a robust alternative — the interquartile range (IQR): the width of the "middle half" of the data, which ignores the extremes. We will see it on a box plot in the next lesson.

To judge whether a spread is large, compare σ with the mean. σ = 3 against a mean of 35 is under 10% — a steady team. σ = 14 against the same mean is almost 40% — salaries are all over the place. The ratio of σ to the mean (the coefficient of variation) lets you compare variability across scales.

definitions.text: how far one value sits from the mean (x̄).
definitions.simple: насколько конкретное значение промахнулось мимо среднего.

definitions.text: the average of squared deviations. It lives in "squared" units, so it is awkward to read directly.
definitions.simple: the average squared miss — spread, but in "squared" units.

definitions.text: the square root of variance — the typical deviation from the mean, in the same units as the data.
definitions.simple: the typical miss from the mean, in normal units.

definitions.text: spread relative to the mean; lets you compare variability of quantities at different scales.
definitions.simple: spread as a percentage of the mean — comparable across scales.

definitions.text: the width of the middle half of the data (from Q1 to Q3); an outlier-robust measure of spread.
definitions.simple: the width of the middle: where the central half of the data lives.

practice: Back to the two pizzerias. Both average 35k, but the first has σ ≈ 3 and the second σ ≈ 14. Same mean — completely different lives: in the first team salaries are predictable, in the second the gap between people is huge.
That is why the center is almost never shown alone. "Mean plus spread" is the minimal honest pair that describes data: where it concentrates and how widely it diverges.
realLife: Spread is everywhere stability matters, not just size. Two couriers both average 30 minutes per delivery, but one lands within 25–35 and the other swings from 10 to 60: same mean, different reliability — and σ shows it instantly.
The same idea underpins stock volatility (it is the σ of returns: the bigger, the riskier) and manufacturing quality control, where going "beyond 3σ" counts as a defect.
Next we will look at the shapes data can take. There the mean and σ play together: for the bell-shaped, "normal" distribution they almost fully define it.
assumptions: The standard deviation is built on the mean, so it inherits its weakness: one strong outlier inflates σ until the "typical spread" no longer describes typical data. For skewed distributions (incomes, response times) IQR or percentiles are more honest.

σ reads well through the 68-95-99.7 rule only for roughly symmetric "bells". For two-humped data (a mix of two different groups) σ describes the emptiness between the humps — look at the shape first, compress it into one number second.
nextLabel: We have the mean and the spread — but what shapes does the data itself take? → distributions and the bell curve
